title: An Irregular Deligne–Simpson Problem
speaker: Zhiwei Yun
abstract: The Deligne--Simpson problem asks for a criterion of the existence of connections on an algebraic curve with prescribed singularities at punctures. We give a solution to a generalization of this problem to $G$-connections on $\mathbb{P}^1$ with a regular singularity and an irregular singularity (satisfying a condition called isoclinic). Here $G$ can be any complex reductive group. Perhaps surprisingly, the solution can be expressed in terms of representations of rational Cherednik algebras. This is joint work with Konstantin Jakob.
tags: Conference on 100 Years of Noetherian Rings

proof_stages.label: Regular Deligne--Simpson as monodromy data
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by formulating the classical Deligne--Simpson problem as a product-one equation for prescribed matrix conjugacy classes, equivalently as existence of a local system or regular-singular connection on a punctured projective line with prescribed local monodromy or residue.

proof_stages.label: Irregular isoclinic G-connection problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews formal irregular connections, cyclic ramified covers, slopes and isoclinic leading terms, then states the two-point irregular Deligne--Simpson problem for a connected reductive group G with slope mu at infinity and prescribed adjoint orbit at 0.

proof_stages.label: Allowed slopes from regular Weyl-group elements
proof_stages.summary: Known GL_n results motivate the local constraint that the denominator of the slope must be the order of a Springer-regular Weyl-group element, with the Q&A later relating this same condition to stable cyclic gradings.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic criterion via rational Cherednik algebras
proof_stages.summary: For elliptic regular denominators, the main algebraic theorem identifies solvability with containment of the Springer representation attached to the nilpotent limit of the residue orbit inside the finite-dimensional rational Cherednik module L_mu(triv).

proof_stages.label: Classical-type explicit nilpotent-orbit classification
proof_stages.summary: For classical groups the general criterion is made explicit by associating to each allowed slope a unique minimal nilpotent orbit O_mu, so solvability is equivalent to the scaled closure of the prescribed orbit containing O_mu.

proof_stages.label: Degeneration of connection moduli to affine Springer fibers
proof_stages.summary: The proof constructs a moduli space of the relevant connections, degenerates it in a Deligne--Simpson/Hitchin-style family to Higgs data, and uses a C*-contraction to reduce existence with prescribed residue to nonemptiness of a condition inside an affine Springer fiber.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological Cherednik detection and classical fixed-point computation
proof_stages.summary: Actions of rational Cherednik-type algebras on affine Springer fiber cohomology convert the affine-Springer nonemptiness condition into the Weyl-representation containment criterion, while explicit torus fixed-point computations in the affine Grassmannian yield the classical tables.

examples.name: Three conjugacy classes with an eigenvalue obstruction
examples.note: The speaker takes two conjugacy classes with two eigenvalues equal to 1 and a third with no eigenvalue 1 to illustrate that the regular Deligne--Simpson matrix equation need not be solvable.
examples.engagement_reason: The three conjugacy classes are set up and the non-solvability is asserted as an exercise, with no proof or computation carried out.

examples.name: Slope-one zero-residue connection
examples.note: For the zero orbit at 0 and slope 1 at infinity, a connection with regular-semisimple leading term is written down to show concretely how an irregular singularity of slope 1 appears.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes an explicit slope-one connection, changes to the coordinate at infinity, and checks the second-order pole, regular semisimple leading term, and absence of other singularity.

examples.engagement_reason: Regular adjoint orbits are cited as an extreme case where the answer is yes for any slope, but this is only stated and not checked on the example.

examples.name: Kloosterman connection at slope 1/n
examples.note: In the GL_n benchmark, the case d=1 forces the regular nilpotent orbit and recovers the Katz--Deligne Kloosterman connection, illustrating one extreme of the known type-A answer.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the type-A criterion at slope 1/n, deriving that the Jordan type must have at most one part and hence gives the regular nilpotent/Kloosterman case.

examples.name: Type-B row of the classical table
examples.note: For G=SO(2n+1), the speaker sketches how allowed denominators and balanced Jordan partitions specify the minimal nilpotent orbit O_mu, giving the flavor of the complete classical-type classification.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker spells out the type-B case by giving allowable denominators and working through cases for the associated Jordan partitions in the classical table.
